items.title: ACL injury mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism of injury to the anterior cruciate ligament

  • The most common mechanism is the deceleration and external rotation of the tibia on the femur with the foot fixed, typical in change of direction sports.
  • Non-contact injury represents 70-80% of cases; forced valgus with external rotation can also tear it.
  • In the deceleration and rotation mechanism, excessive stress on the anterior cruciate ligament exceeds its resistance (approximately 2000 N), causing complete or partial rupture.
  • Isolated injury to the anterior cruciate ligament is rare in direct impacts; It is usually combined with meniscal or medial collateral ligament injuries.

items.foco: O'Donoghue triad: ACL, LCI, medial meniscus
items.title: O'Donoghue triad
items.contentHtml:
O'Donoghue Triad

  • The O'Donoghue triad (or unhappy triad) includes injury to the anterior cruciate ligament, medial collateral ligament and medial meniscus.
  • The classic mechanism is a valgus blow on the knee in flexion with external rotation of the tibia, common in sports contact.
  • Injury to the medial collateral ligament usually occurs in its superficial portion; The medial meniscus is torn by compression between the femoral condyle and the tibial plateau.
  • Clinically it presents with acute effusion, anteromedial instability and pain in the medial interline; requires MRI evaluation to plan surgery.

items.foco: Lachman test: more sensitive for ACL
items.title: Lachman test
items.contentHtml:
Lachman test

  • The Lachman test is the most sensitive (85-95%) to evaluate the integrity of the anterior cruciate ligament.
  • It is performed with the patient in the supine position, knee flexed 20-30°; The examiner stabilizes the femur with one hand and moves the tibia anteriorly with the other.
  • Excessive anterior displacement and the absence of a firm end point indicate rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament.
  • Compared with the anterior drawer test (sensitivity 50-60%), the Lachman test is superior because it minimizes the influence of muscle contraction and the position of the meniscus.

items.foco: Anterior drawer: evaluates ACL in 90° flexion
items.title: Previous drawer test
items.contentHtml:
Anterior drawer test

  • The anterior drawer test evaluates the stability of the anterior cruciate ligament with the knee flexed at 90° and the foot stabilized.
  • The examiner pulls the tibia anteriorly; a displacement >6 mm with respect to the contralateral side suggests rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament. A positive result must be confirmed with MRI.

items.foco: McMurray test: diagnosis of meniscal injury
items.title: McMurray test
items.contentHtml:
McMurray test

  • The McMurray test is the classic maneuver to diagnose injuries to the medial and lateral meniscus.
  • With the patient in the supine position, the knee is flexed to the maximum; then the knee is extended while external and internal rotation of the tibia is applied with valgus or varus stress.
  • An audible or palpable clicking accompanied by pain in the joint interline is positive for a meniscal tear.
  • The sensitivity of the McMurray test ranges between 50-70% and the specificity is high (80-90%) when compared with arthroscopic findings.

items.foco: Joint block: suggests meniscal bucket handle
items.title: Bucket handle joint block
items.contentHtml:
Joint lock and bucket handle

  • The joint lock (loss of complete extension >10°) is a clinical sign that suggests a bucket handle meniscal tear.
  • In this type of injury, the displaced meniscal fragment is interposed between the femoral condyle and the tibial plateau, preventing extension terminal of the knee.
  • It is more common in the medial meniscus due to its lower mobility and greater fixation to the joint capsule.
  • Acute joint block requires urgent arthroscopic reduction to avoid chondral damage; MRI confirms the “bucket handle” pattern with the “double posterior cruciate ligament” sign.

items.foco: Medial vs lateral meniscal injury: medial most common
items.title: Frequency of medial vs lateral meniscal injury
items.contentHtml:
Medial vs lateral meniscus injury

  • Injury to the medial meniscus is 2-3 times more common than that of the lateral meniscus due to its lower mobility and greater fixation to the capsule and the medial collateral ligament.
  • The medial meniscus supports 50% of the load in the medial compartment, while the lateral meniscus supports 70% in the lateral compartment; This explains the higher incidence of medial tears in degenerative injuries.
  • Injuries to the lateral meniscus are usually associated with acute tears of the anterior cruciate ligament, while those of the medial meniscus are more common in degenerative or traumatic contexts with valgus.
  • The differential symptoms include pain in the medial vs. lateral interline, and the McMurray test can lateralize the snap.

items.foco: Steinmann I sign: pain in the medial interline when rotating
items.title: Steinmann I sign
items.contentHtml:
Steinmann I sign

  • The Steinmann I sign consists of pain referred to the medial joint interline when externally rotating the tibia with the knee flexed at 90°.
  • This finding suggests injury to the medial meniscus; external rotation stretches the medial meniscus, reproducing the pain.
  • There is also the Steinmann sign II, where the pain moves posteriorly when the knee is flexed, indicating a tear of the posterior horn of the meniscus.
  • The sensitivity of the Steinmann sign I is moderate (~60%) and should be interpreted together with other maneuvers such as the McMurray test and MRI. magnetic.

items.foco: Late joint effusion: suggests meniscal injury
items.title: Late joint effusion in meniscal injury
items.contentHtml:
Delayed joint effusion

  • Delayed joint effusion (appearance 6-24 hours after trauma) is characteristic of meniscal injuries, unlike immediate effusion (<2 hours) that suggests rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament or fracture.
  • The delay is due to the fact that reactive synovitis and the production of synovial fluid require time after irritation. mechanics of the meniscal fragment.
  • In chronic meniscal injuries, the effusion can be recurrent and of small volume, associated with episodes of blockage or instability.
  • The presence of late joint effusion without a high-energy mechanism points to degenerative or mild traumatic meniscal pathology.

items.title: Rotational instability and posterolateral corner
items.contentHtml:
Rotational instability due to posterolateral corner injury

  • The rotational instability of the knee, especially in external rotation, suggests injury to the posterolateral corner (ligamentary complex that includes the popliteal fibular ligament, the popliteal tendon and the lateral collateral ligament).
  • It is evaluated with the external rotation test (test of Dial) at 30° and 90° flexion; an asymmetric increase in external rotation >10° indicates injury.
  • Isolated injury to the posterolateral corner is rare; It is usually associated with a rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament or the posterior cruciate ligament, generating multidirectional instability.
  • Not diagnosing it leads to failure of the reconstruction of the anterior cruciate ligament; MRI is essential to identify damage to these structures.

items.foco: MRI: gold standard for menisci and ACL
items.title: MRI in knee injuries
items.contentHtml:
MRI: gold standard

  • MRI is the non-invasive gold standard for evaluating injuries of the anterior cruciate ligament and menisci, with sensitivity >90% and specificity >95%.
  • In rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament, direct signs include fiber discontinuity and abnormal signal on T2 sequences; Indirect signs are the double posterior cruciate ligament sign (anterior displacement of the tibia) and the Segond fracture.
  • For menisci, the double posterior cruciate ligament sign (bucket handle) and the presence of an intrameniscal signal that contacts the articular surface (grade III in Stoller's classification) are looked for.
  • Magnetic resonance imaging also detects associated injuries such as contusions. bone (pivot shift pattern) and chondral damage.

items.foco: Early vs late ACL reconstruction: late reduces osteoarthritis
items.title: Early vs late ACL reconstruction
items.contentHtml:
Early vs late reconstruction of the anterior cruciate ligament

  • Early reconstruction (within the first 3 weeks) of the anterior cruciate ligament is associated with a greater risk of arthrofibrosis and postoperative joint stiffness.
  • Late reconstruction (after 6-8 weeks, after regaining full range of motion and stroke control) reduces the incidence of long-term osteoarthritis, by allowing adequate preoperative rehabilitation.
  • Comparative studies show that late reconstruction does not increase the rate of secondary meniscal injuries if adequate prior functional stabilization is performed.
  • The decision should be individualized according to the level of activity, residual instability and associated injuries; In elite athletes, early surgery with an intensive rehabilitation protocol may be chosen.
